children.name: Phoca
children.description: Harbour and spotted seals — small, mottled seals of the cold coastal waters of the North Atlantic and North Pacific. They are among the most familiar seals, hauling out on rocky shores, sandbars, and ice floes. Unlike sea lions, true seals cannot rotate their hind flippers to walk; on land they move with an undulating caterpillar motion, but in water they are swift and graceful, steering with their hind flippers. The Harbour Seal is one of the few seals regularly found in fresh water.

children.name: Phoca vitulina
children.description: The most widely distributed pinniped, found on both shores of the North Atlantic and North Pacific. Their dog-like face and inquisitive nature make them a favourite of coastal wildlife observers.

children.name: Pusa
children.description: Ringed, Caspian, and Baikal seals — the three smallest phocid species. The Ringed Seal is the most abundant seal in the Arctic, the principal prey of the Polar Bear, and the only seal capable of maintaining a breathing hole through thick sea ice using the claws of its foreflippers. The Baikal Seal is the world's only exclusively freshwater pinniped, isolated in Lake Baikal since the Pleistocene and now one of the smallest seal species.

children.name: Pusa hispida
children.description: The Arctic's most abundant seal and the staple food of Polar Bears. Pups are born in snow lairs — subnivean structures dug by the mother that hide newborns from predators for up to six weeks.

children.name: Pusa sibirica
children.description: The world's only fully freshwater seal, endemic to Russia's Lake Baikal. How ancestors reached the landlocked lake remains debated; they likely entered via ancient river connections during the Pleistocene.

children.name: Pusa caspica
children.description: Endemic to the Caspian Sea. Classified as Endangered; population declined roughly 90% over the 20th century due to hunting, disease, and pollution.

children.name: Halichoerus
children.description: The Grey Seal — one of the largest seals in the world and the species most familiar to coastal visitors in the British Isles. Males ('bulls') can exceed 300 kg and bear the scarred, arched snout ('horse-head') profile characteristic of the genus. Breeding colonies in autumn are raucous affairs: bulls fight for space on beaches to access cows, and the pups, born white and fluffy, moult into a silver coat within three weeks.

children.name: Halichoerus grypus
children.description: About 40% of the world population breeds around the coasts of Britain and Ireland. The species was commercially hunted for centuries and is now a key indicator of healthy fish stocks.

children.name: Erignathus
children.description: The Bearded Seal — named for its conspicuous, dense white moustache of squared-off whiskers, which are exquisitely sensitive mechanoreceptors for detecting invertebrates under sediment. It is the largest of the Arctic seals outside the elephant seals, and is a critical food source for polar bears, walruses, and Arctic peoples. Males produce eerie, spiralling underwater songs that carry for kilometres and play a role in mate attraction.

children.name: Mirounga
children.description: Elephant seals — the largest pinnipeds on Earth. Southern elephant seal bulls can weigh 3,700 kg. Both species are sexually dimorphic to an extreme degree: males are four to five times heavier than females, and mature males bear the inflatable proboscis that gives the genus its common name. They are deep divers, capable of plunging to 2,000 metres and holding their breath for up to two hours, their heart rate dropping to four beats per minute. Once hunted almost to extinction for their oil, both species have recovered substantially.

children.name: Mirounga angustirostris
children.description: Reduced to as few as 20–100 individuals by the 1890s before hunting ceased; the population has since recovered to over 170,000. A textbook example of a genetic bottleneck and population recovery.

children.name: Mirounga leonina
children.description: The largest pinniped species. Bulls engage in violent beach battles for harems; dominant bulls (beachmasters) may sire up to 500 pups over their lifetime.

children.name: Leptonychotes
children.description: The Weddell Seal — the most southerly-breeding mammal in the world, nesting on fast ice adjacent to the Antarctic continent. It is the best-studied wild seal, primarily through long-running research at McMurdo Station. Weddell Seals maintain breathing holes through sea ice using their teeth and are capable of dives exceeding 600 metres — navigating back to the same breathing hole in complete darkness beneath the ice by echolocation and memory.

children.name: Hydrurga
children.description: The Leopard Seal — apex predator of the Antarctic pack ice, named for its spotted coat and its fearsome hunting prowess. It is the only seal that routinely preys on warm-blooded animals other than fish, including penguins and the pups of other seal species. It has the widest jaw gape relative to body size of any seal, and its back teeth are tri-lobed sieves that also allow it to strain krill from water. Individuals have attacked — and in one documented case killed — humans.

children.name: Lobodon
children.description: The Crabeater Seal — the most abundant pinniped on Earth, with a population estimated between 7 and 75 million, inhabiting the pack ice surrounding Antarctica. Despite the name, it does not eat crabs; its tri-lobed teeth interlock to form a sieve for filtering Antarctic krill, which make up 98% of its diet. The high abundance of Crabeater Seals is a direct consequence of the collapse of great whale populations, which released enormous quantities of krill from predation — a phenomenon known as the 'krill surplus'.

children.name: Lobodon carcinophaga
children.description: Almost all adults bear parallel scars from failed Leopard Seal attacks during their first year — a mute record of predation pressure. Estimated to consume more krill annually than all great whales combined.

children.name: Monachus
children.description: Mediterranean and Caribbean monk seals — among the most critically endangered pinnipeds in the world. The Caribbean Monk Seal was declared extinct in 2008 after no confirmed sighting since 1952, making it the first pinniped to go extinct in historical times in the Atlantic. The Mediterranean Monk Seal persists with fewer than 700 individuals, mainly around Madeira, the Ionian coast, and Mauritania.

children.name: Monachus monachus
children.description: One of the world's rarest marine mammals. Once common throughout the Mediterranean and Black Sea, now restricted to scattered small colonies. Pups are born in sea caves to avoid human disturbance.

children.name: Neomonachus
children.description: Hawaiian Monk Seal — an ancient lineage that diverged from its Atlantic relatives around 3–4 million years ago as the Central American Seaway closed. Endemic to the Hawaiian Islands, it is one of the most endangered marine mammals in the world. The population was devastated by hunting for skins, meat, and oil in the 19th century and declined further through the 20th century due to entanglement in fishing gear and male harassment of females. Conservation efforts since the 1980s have stabilised the population at around 1,400 individuals.

children.name: Neomonachus schauinslandi
children.description: Endemic to the Hawaiian archipelago. Considered a sacred aumakua (guardian spirit) in Hawaiian culture. Critical threats today include entanglement, disease, and rising sea levels swamping pupping beaches.
